shapes.title: Target table not found
shapes.description: ADD COLUMN, ALTER COLUMN, DROP COLUMN, or similar operation references a table that does not exist in the replayed schema state.

corpus_suitability.reason: Production-merged filters out hallucinated table references

shapes.title: Target column not found
shapes.description: Operation references a column that does not exist on the target table in the replayed schema state.

corpus_suitability.reason: Production-merged filters out hallucinated column references

shapes.title: Foreign key references unknown table or column
shapes.description: FK constraint references a table or column that does not exist in the replayed schema state.

corpus_suitability.reason: Production-merged filters out unknown FK targets

shapes.title: Create target already exists
shapes.description: CREATE TABLE or CREATE INDEX targets a name that already exists in the replayed schema state.

corpus_suitability.reason: Production-merged filters out duplicate create attempts

shapes.title: Rename source missing or target conflict
shapes.description: RENAME TABLE or RENAME COLUMN source does not exist, or the target name already exists.

corpus_suitability.reason: Production-merged filters out broken rename operations

shapes.title: DROP COLUMN with FK dependents
shapes.description: DROP COLUMN targets a column that has incoming foreign key references from other tables, which will cascade or fail at runtime.

tier_history.reason: calibration attempted, produced 0 findings on prisma-production-v1, below 10-finding floor

calibration attempted on django-readthedocs-v1 via new Django AST parser, produced 0 canonical incoming-FK findings and 4 outgoing-FK-sub-case findings (all FP due to parser not modeling Django's implicit DROP CONSTRAINT before DROP COLUMN); 4 below the 10-finding floor

corpus_suitability.reason: Prisma migration generator emits DROP CONSTRAINT before DROP COLUMN, so by the time DROP COLUMN runs the FK is already gone

Canonical DM-15 sub-case (incoming-FK dependents) produced 0 findings across 345 migrations; 72 RemoveField operations exist but none target a column with live incoming FK references at drop time. Outgoing-FK warning sub-case fires 4 times but all are parser-modeling artifacts, not true DM-15 failures — see attempt dm15-django-readthedocs-v1-2026-04-14 in attempts.jsonl.

shapes.title: DROP TABLE with FK dependents
shapes.description: DROP TABLE targets a table that has incoming foreign key references from other tables.

tier_history.reason: calibration attempted, produced 0 findings on prisma-production-v1, below 10-finding floor

corpus_suitability.reason: Prisma migration generator drops FK constraints before DROP TABLE, same defensive sequence as DM-15

shapes.title: Column type change is narrowing
shapes.description: ALTER COLUMN TYPE converts to a narrower type, causing potential data loss (BIGINT to INT, TEXT to VARCHAR(N), TIMESTAMPTZ to DATE, NUMERIC to FLOAT4).

corpus_suitability.reason: Prisma computes type changes through intermediate columns rather than direct narrowings

known_issues: Hardcoded narrowing-types table is incomplete. Needs expansion before calibration on any corpus.

shapes.title: NOT NULL without safe preconditions
shapes.description: ADD COLUMN x NOT NULL without DEFAULT, or ALTER COLUMN x SET NOT NULL where x is currently nullable and has no default. Will fail on any non-empty table at runtime. Scope note: DM-18 fires on the *structural* missing-value case only. It does NOT fire on ADD COLUMN NOT NULL WITH DEFAULT — since Postgres 11, non-volatile DEFAULTs are stored in table metadata without rewriting rows, so the migration is fast and structurally safe. Volatile DEFAULTs (now(), gen_random_uuid(), subqueries) still force a table rewrite, but that's a separate shape concern (table-rewrite cost) not covered by DM-18. Reference: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-altertable.html notes section.

tier_history.reason: 19/0 precision on prisma-production-v1, clears 80% blocking threshold and 10-finding sample floor

corpus_suitability.reason: NOT NULL without DEFAULT is a runtime data failure that no generator can pre-empt and code review cannot catch without knowing row counts. Fired on 19 of 761 migrations (2.5% base rate).

provenance_notes: DM-18's classification work (recorded in dad8a42 at 09:29 on 2026-04-12) predates the formal calibration bar commit (2b5d1fb at 17:20 on 2026-04-12) by approximately seven hours and fifty-one minutes. DM-18 is therefore calibrated under a retroactively-formalized bar. The promotion decision matches what the bar would have required (19/19 = 100% clears every threshold), but the procedural pre-registration requirement of Component 1 was not satisfied for this specific claim. This gap is acknowledged in commit 51d767d (2026-04-13). The first shape measured under a fully pre-registered bar is DM-15.

shapes.title: DROP INDEX backing a constraint
shapes.description: DROP INDEX targets an index that is the backing index for a UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY constraint, which would break the constraint invariant.

corpus_suitability.reason: Prisma manages indexes through named constraint definitions, so DROP INDEX on a constraint-backing index is pre-empted

known_issues: Index-name matching is fragile. Needs hardening before calibration on any corpus.

shapes.title: SET NOT NULL without application-compatibility window (deploy-window race)
shapes.description: SET NOT NULL (or ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL with DEFAULT) on an existing table where application code does not yet tolerate the invariant. The migration completes successfully and existing rows satisfy the constraint, but new writes from application code still running a pre-migration revision fail because they do not provide a value for the column. Distinct from DM-18 (which fires on migrations that cannot even execute against non-empty tables) — DM-28 fires on migrations that execute cleanly and then break writes during the deploy window between migration completion and application code rollout.

implementation.notes: Not a per-file CI gate. Takes a whole MigrationSequence, extracts SET NOT NULL and ADD COLUMN NOT NULL DEFAULT events, and scans forward up to 30 migrations for DROP NOT NULL reverts on the same table.column. Only fires on confirmed revert pairs. A static per-file heuristic form for speculative DM-28 risk is not implemented and is not part of this shape's current form.

tier_history.reason: Promoted from prose-only to designed-tier after Path B full-corpus revert discovery scan cleared the pre-locked DM-28 threshold from path-b-scoping-draft.md (two independent incidents of the safe-then-reverted deploy-window shape).

Registry honesty: detector implemented in scripts/mvp-migration/deploy-window-gate.ts and re-derived both cited incidents plus one sibling denormalized-table case (3 findings / 210 SET-NOT-NULL events on the 761-migration Prisma corpus, 1.4% retrospective base rate). Moved from designed to shipped so the registry reflects code reality. Explicitly uncalibrated: 3 findings is below the 10-finding sample-size floor and no precision number is claimed. Severity stays warning; CI behavior stays retrospective-audit-only, not a default warning gate.

evidence.notes: Original DM-28 hypothesis incident from Path C. Team added NOT NULL to uuid column using a safe three-step pattern, then reverted NOT NULL in a follow-up migration while keeping the column and backfilled data. Revert signature is consistent with new writes from pre-migration application code failing during the deploy window.

Second independent incident found by Path B on 2026-04-14. Different table, different feature area (teams vs routing forms), different time period (2022-10 vs 2025-06) from the first incident. Original migration carried a Prisma 'This step will fail if there are existing NULL values' warning and was shipped anyway; 27 days later a revert dropped NOT NULL on slug while the migration's stated purpose was to add unrelated subscription columns (createdAt, metadata). The revert being buried in an unrelated-purpose migration is the production-pressure-fix signature (not a same-day developer hotfix, not a years-later planned refactor, but the characteristic 'lived in prod for a few weeks, caused write failures, got quietly patched in the next touching migration' window).

shapes.distinction_from_dm25: DM-25 (designed, not yet promoted here) covers SET NOT NULL failures that manifest at migration execution time on non-empty tables without defaults. DM-28 covers SET NOT NULL that executes cleanly (either the column was nullable-to-NOT NULL with a completed backfill, or ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL DEFAULT with a working default) but breaks new writes during the deploy window because application code running an older revision does not yet provide the value. DM-25 is a migration-execution failure; DM-28 is a deploy-coordination failure. A single migration can satisfy DM-25 without being vulnerable to DM-28 (if application code is simultaneously updated), and a single migration can be vulnerable to DM-28 without triggering DM-25 (if the column has a safe backfill but the application deploy lags).
shapes.distinction_from_dm18: DM-18 (calibrated, shipped, error-severity) covers ADD COLUMN NOT NULL without DEFAULT and SET NOT NULL where the column has no default and is currently nullable — both of which will fail at migration execution time on a non-empty table. DM-28 covers the case where the migration succeeds. The failure mode is different (runtime writes failing post-migration vs the migration itself failing to execute) and the fix is different (coordinate application deploy vs add a default or backfill).
known_issues: Not yet implemented. No gate file, no function. Designed-tier means the hypothesis is documented and the evidence meets the threshold; implementation and calibration are future work.

The detection signal requires looking forward from a migration to the application deploy window and backward from a revert to the originating migration — neither of which the existing single-file safety gates can do. A gate for DM-28 will need corpus-level or sequence-level analysis, not per-file checking.

Precision claims for DM-28 cannot come from production-merged corpora alone. The shape's suitable corpus class is revert-derived/pre-fix states where the 'broken deploy window was painful enough to trigger a revert' signal is on disk. The pre-fix pipeline v1 queued as follow-up work is the intended corpus class.
